passages.passageHtml:
[1] There is not a law under which any part of the universe is
governed that does not come into play and is touched upon in
the chemistry of a candle. It is a little thing, yet it leads us into
the deepest questions of science. Let us begin with the flame
[5] itself.

You see before you a candle burning quietly. What supports this
flame? Where does it come from? What are its sources? The first
thing to notice is that the candle does not burn all at once like
a piece of wood thrown into the fire. Instead, the flame clings
[10] to the wick, and the wax around it melts slowly into a little cup
or basin. This melting is important. The heat of the flame melts
the solid wax, turning it into liquid, which rises up the wick by
capillary action—just as oil rises in a lamp. If you look closely,
you will see that the wick is surrounded by this molten wax,
[15] which creeps up and feeds the flame.

Now, blow out the candle for a moment. You will notice a stream
of white vapor rising from the wick. This vapor is the combustible
part of the wax that was vaporized but not yet burned. If you
bring a lighted match near it quickly, the vapor catches fire, and
[20] the flame runs down to relight the wick. This shows that the
flame is not in the wick alone, nor in the solid wax, but in the
vapor rising from it. The candle, then, furnishes a stream of
combustible vapor, which mixes with the air and burns.

Examine the flame more carefully. It has a definite shape and
[25] structure. At the base, near the wick, it is dark and cooler.
Higher up, it becomes bright yellow or white, and at the outer edge, there
is a pale blue mantle. The brightness comes from solid particles of
carbon glowing with heat. These particles are produced in the flame
itself. In the inner part, where air cannot easily reach, the vapor

[30] is not fully burned, and tiny bits of carbon are set free.
These carbon particles become white-hot and give the flame its light.
But at the outer edge, where plenty of air mixes in,
the carbon burns completely to an invisible gas, and there you see the
blue color of complete combustion.

[35] What happens to the wax? It does not disappear into nothing. If we
capture the products of the burning, we find two main things: water
and carbonic acid (what we now call carbon dioxide). To show the
water, hold a cold spoon or a piece of glass over the flame. Soon it
becomes clouded with moisture, and drops of water collect. This water
[40] comes from the hydrogen in the wax combining with oxygen from the air.

The wax is a compound of carbon and hydrogen—a hydrocarbon—and
when it burns, the hydrogen unites with oxygen to form water.
The other product is carbonic acid gas. If we pour limewater into a jar
and blow the products of the flame through it (or let the candle burn
[45] inside a closed vessel until the air is used up), the limewater turns
milky. This milkiness is caused by carbon dioxide combining with the
lime to form chalk. Thus, the carbon from the wax joins with oxygen
from the air to produce this gas.

See how beautifully everything is balanced. The air supplies oxygen;
[50] the candle supplies carbon and hydrogen. The products are water vapor
and carbon dioxide, which pass into the atmosphere. Nothing is lost—
only changed. The same laws that govern the burning of a candle govern
the burning of coal, oil, or gas, and even the breathing of animals.
In respiration, we take oxygen from the air, combine it with carbon
[55] from our food, and give out carbon dioxide and water—just as the
candle does.

In this simple candle flame, then, we have a miniature world of
chemical change: melting, vaporizing, combining, glowing, and producing
new substances. It teaches us that science begins with careful observation
[60] of the commonest things around us.

[1] The share which worms have taken in the formation of the
layer of vegetable mould, which covers the whole surface of the
land in every moderately humid country, is the subject of the
present volume. This layer, commonly called vegetable mould or
[5] humus, consists of disintegrated and decomposed organic matter
mixed with fine particles of mineral soil. It is the fertile topsoil
in which plants thrive, and its gradual accumulation has long
puzzled observers.

Worms, though lowly creatures, appear to play a major part
[10] in this process. They swallow earth in large quantities for the sake
of the organic matter it contains—decaying leaves, roots, and
other vegetable debris. After digestion, they eject the indigestible
portion as castings. These castings are brought to the surface and
spread out in thin layers, slowly building up the mould over time.
[15] In many places, the castings are so abundant that they form small
heaps or towers, especially after rain, when the worms are most
active.

I have estimated that in ordinary garden soil, the number of
worms may reach several millions per acre. Each worm swallows
[20] and ejects earth repeatedly. One observer calculated that on good
pasture land, the annual amount of earth thus brought to the
surface might equal a layer more than two inches thick over the
whole area. Though this figure seems high, repeated observations
confirm that worms pass vast quantities of soil through their
[25] bodies every year. Over centuries, this action must produce a thick
layer of mould.

The castings themselves are finely divided. The earth, having
passed through the worm's gizzard and intestine, is ground up and
mixed with digestive fluids. This process not only aerates the soil
[30] but also brings up mineral particles from below, blending them
with organic remains on the surface. Stones and larger objects are
gradually buried as castings accumulate around and over them. In
fields long undisturbed, large stones sink slowly into the earth,
covered by the mould formed through worm activity.

[35] Worms also affect the surface in other ways. They drag
leaves, petioles, and small twigs into their burrows, partly to plug
the openings against cold or enemies, and partly for food.
Triangles of paper or thin leaves placed near burrow mouths are
often drawn in during the night. The worms show some judgment
[40] in how they seize objects—generally pulling them by the base or
middle to fit the burrow entrance more neatly. This habit aids in
covering the ground with vegetable debris, which then decays and
contributes to the mould.

Burrows themselves are lined with castings, making smooth,
[45] firm walls. The worms line them to prevent collapse and to keep
the interior damp. At night, especially in warm weather, worms
come to the surface to feed or to mate, moving with surprising
speed and leaving trails on lawns or paths. Their activity is
greatest after rain or in mild, moist conditions; in dry or cold
[50] weather, they retire deep into the soil.

The action of worms is slow but continuous. In the course of
ages, they have leveled irregularities on the surface, buried
ruins, and transformed barren subsoil into rich vegetable mould.
Even rocks are gradually disintegrated as humus acids from
[55] decaying matter aid the process, and worms accelerate the mixing.
Thus, these small, silent laborers, unnoticed by most, have exerted
a powerful influence on the earth's history, creating the fertile
layer upon which all terrestrial life depends.

[1] The problem of the twentieth century is the problem of the color
line, the relation of the darker to the lighter races of men in Asia
and Africa, in America and the islands of the sea. It was a phase of
this problem that caused the Civil War; and however much they who
[5] marched South and North in 1861 may have fixed on the technical
points, of union and local autonomy as a shibboleth, all nevertheless
knew, as we know, that the question of Negro slavery was the real
cause of the conflict. Curious it was, too, how this deeper question
ever forced itself to the surface despite effort and disclaimer.
[10] No sooner had Northern armies touched Southern soil than this old
question, newly quised, sprang from the earth, What shall be done
with Negroes? Peremptory military commands this way and that,
could not answer the query; the Emancipation Proclamation seemed
but to broaden and intensify the difficulties; and the War Amendments
[15] made the Negro problems of to-day.

It is the aim of this essay to study the period of history from 1861 to
1872 so far as it relates to the American Negro. In effect, this tale of
the dawn of Freedom is an account of that government of men called
the Freedmen's Bureau, one of the most singular and interesting of
[20] the attempts made by a great nation to grapple with vast problems
of race and social condition.

The war has naught to do with slaves, cried Congress, the President,
and the Nation; and yet no sooner had the armies, East and West,
penetrated Virginia and Tennessee than fugitive slaves appeared
[25] within their lines. They came at night, when the flickering camp
fires shone like vast unsteady stars along the black horizon: old men
and thin, with gray and tufted hair; women with frightened eyes,
dragging whimpering hungry children; men and girls, stalwart and
gaunt, a- a horde of starving vagabonds, homeless, helpless, and
[30] pitiable, in their dark distress. Two methods of treating these
newcomers seemed equally logical to opposite sorts of minds.

Ben Butler, in Virginia, quickly declared slave property contraband
of war, and put the fugitives to work; while Fremont, in Missouri,
declared the slaves free under martial law. Butler's action was
[35] approved, but Fremont's was hastily countermanded, and
his successor, Halleck, saw things differently. "Hereafter," he
commanded, "no slaves should be allowed to come into your lines at
all; if any come without your knowledge, when owners call for them
deliver them." Such a policy was difficult to enforce; some of the
[40] black refugees declared themselves freemen, others showed that
their masters had deserted them, and still others were captured with
forts and plantations. Evidently, too, slaves were a source of strength
to the Confederacy, and were being used as laborers and producers.

"They constitute a military resource," wrote Secretary Cameron, late
[45] in 1861; "and being such, that they should not be turned over to the
enemy is too plain to discuss." So gradually the tone of the army
chiefs changed; Congress forbade the rendition of fugitives, and
Butler's "contrabands" were welcomed as military laborers. This
complicated rather than solved the problem, for now the scattering
[50] fugitives became a steady stream, which flowed faster as the armies
marched.

Then the long- headed man with care- chiselled face who sat in the
White House saw the inevitable, and emancipated the slaves of rebels
on New Year's, 1863. A month later Congress called earnestly for the
[55] Negro soldiers whom the act of July, 1862, had half grudgingly
allowed to enlist. Thus the barriers were levelled and the deed was
done. The stream of fugitives swelled to a flood, and anxious army
officers kept inquiring: "What must be done with slaves, arriving
almost daily? Are we to find food and shelter for women and
[60] children?"

It was a Pierce of Boston who pointed out the way, and thus became
in a sense the founder of the Freedmen's Bureau. He was a firm friend
of Secretary Chase; and when, in 1861, the care of slaves and
abandoned lands devolved upon the Treasury officials, Pierce was
[65] specially detailed from the ranks to study the conditions. First, he
cared for the refugees at Fortress Monroe; and then, after Sherman
had captured Hilton Head, Pierce was sent there to found his Port
Royal experiment of making free workingmen out of slaves. Before
his experiment was barely started, however, the problem of the
[70] fugitives had assumed such proportions that it was taken from the
hands of the over- burdened Treasury Department and given to the
army officials. Already centres of massed freedmen were forming at
Fortress Monroe, Washington, New Orleans, Vicksburg and Corinth,
Columbus, Ky., and Cairo, Ill., as well as at Port Royal. Army
[75] chaplains found here new and fruitful fields; "superintendents of
contrabands" multiplied, and some attempt at systematic work was
made by enlisting the able- bodied men and giving work to the others.
Then came the Freedmen's Aid societies, born of the touching appeals
from Pierce and from these other centres of distress. There was the
[80] American Missionary Association, sprung from the Amistad, and now
full- grown for work; the various church organizations, the National
Freedmen's Relief Association, the American Freedmen's Union, the
Western Freedmen's Aid Commission, in all fifty or more active
organizations, which sent clothes, money, school- books, and teachers
[85] southward. All they did was needed, for the destitution of the
freedmen was often reported as "too appalling for belief," and the
situation was daily growing worse rather than better.

And daily, too, it seemed more plain that this was no ordinary matter
of temporary relief, but a national crisis; for here loomed a labor
[90] problem of vast dimensions. Masses of Negroes stood idle, or, if they
worked spasmodically, were never sure of pay; and if perchance they
received pay, squandered the new thing thoughtlessly. In these and
other ways were camp- life and the new liberty demoralizing the
freedmen. The broader economic organization thus clearly demanded
[95] sprang up here and there as accident and local conditions determined.

[1] YOU don't know about me without you have read a book by
the name of The Adventures of Tom Sawyer; but that ain't no
matter. That book was made by Mr. Mark Twain, and he told
the truth, mainly. There was things which he stretched, but
[5] mainly he told the truth. That is nothing. I never seen
anybody but lied one time or another, without it was Aunt
Polly, or the widow, or maybe Mary. Aunt Polly - Tom's Aunt
Polly, she is - and Mary, and the Widow Douglas is all told
about in that book, which is mostly a true book, with some
[10] stretchers, as I said before.

Now the way that the book winds up is this: Tom and me
found the money that the robbers hid in the cave, and it
made us rich. We got six thousand dollars apiece - all gold.
It was an awful sight of money when it was piled up. Well,
[15] Judge Thatcher he took it and put it out at interest, and it
fetched us a dollar a day apiece all the year round - more
than a body could tell what to do with. The Widow Douglas
she took me for her son, and allowed she would sivilize me;
but it was rough living in the house all the time, considering
[20] how dismal regular and decent the widow was in all her ways;
and so when I couldn't stand it no longer I lit out.
I got into my old rags and my sugar-hogshead again, and was
free and satisfied. But Tom Sawyer he hunted me up and
said he was going to start a band of robbers, and I might join
[25] if I would go back to the widow and be respectable. So I went back.
The widow she cried over me, and called me a poor lost lamb,
and she called me a lot of other names, too, but she never
meant no harm by it. She put me in them new clothes again,
and I couldn't do nothing but sweat and sweat, and feel all
[30] cramped up. Well, then, the old thing commenced again. The
widow rung a bell for supper, and you had to come to time.

When you got to the table you couldn't go right to eating,
but you had to wait for the widow to tuck down her head and
grumble a little over the victuals, though there wasn't really
[35] anything the matter with them, - that is, nothing only every
thing was cooked by itself. In a barrel of odds and ends it
is different; things get mixed up, and the juice kind of swaps
around, and the things go better.

After supper she got out her book and learned me about Moses
[40] and the Bulrushers, and I was in a sweat to find out all about
him; but by and by she let it out that Moses had been dead a
considerable long time; so then I didn't care no more about
him, because I don't take no stock in dead people.

Pretty soon I wanted to smoke, and asked the widow to let me.
[45] But she wouldn't. She said it was a mean practice and wasn't
clean, and I must try to not do it any more. That is just the
way with some people. They get down on a thing when they don't
know nothing about it. Here she was a-bothering about Moses,
which was no kin to her, and no use to anybody, being gone,
[50] you see, yet finding a power of fault with me for doing a thing
that had some good in it. And she took snuff, too; of course
that was all right, because she done it herself.

questions.questionHtml: In the context of the passage, the main purpose of the third paragraph (lines 16-23) is to:

options.A: propose a theory about the composition of wax.
options.B: illustrate the dangers of relighting a candle with a match.
options.C: demonstrate that the flame burns from a vapor, not from solid wax.
options.D: explain the role of capillary action in feeding the flame.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph describes blowing out the candle and then relighting it from the rising white vapor. The author explicitly states the conclusion: 'This shows that the flame is not in the wick alone, nor in the solid wax, but in the vapor rising from it.' This directly supports option C. Option A is incorrect; the composition is discussed later. Option B is incorrect; safety is not the focus. Option D is incorrect; capillary action is explained in the second paragraph, not the third.

questions.questionHtml: According to the narrator, the bright yellow or white light produced by a candle flame is a direct result of:

options.A: the complete combustion of wax vapor at the outer edge.
options.B: the combination of hydrogen in the wax with oxygen from the air.
options.C: the dark and cooler zone of air at the base of the wick.
options.D: the presence of tiny, intensely heated carbon particles.

questions.explanationHtml: The fourth paragraph provides this explicit detail. Lines 26-28 state, 'Higher up, it becomes bright yellow or white... The brightness comes from solid particles of carbon glowing with heat.' Option D directly matches this statement. Option A describes the blue mantle, not the bright yellow. Option B describes the formation of water. Option C describes a different part of the flame that is not the source of brightness.

questions.questionHtml: It can be reasonably inferred from the passage that the author would most likely describe the relationship between a candle burning and human breathing as:

options.A: coincidental, as both are simple physical processes.
options.B: analogous, as both involve combining a fuel with oxygen to produce similar byproducts.
options.C: identical, as both consume the exact same materials from the air.
options.D: unrelated, as one is a chemical process and the other is a biological one.

questions.explanationHtml: The author makes a direct comparison in lines 52-56: 'The same laws... govern the burning of a candle... and even the breathing of animals. In respiration, we take oxygen from the air, combine it with carbon from our food, and give out carbon dioxide and water—just as the candle does.' This draws an analogy between the two processes. Option C is incorrect because the 'fuel' is different (candle wax vs. food). Options A and D are incorrect because the author explicitly connects them.

questions.questionHtml: The main point of the eighth paragraph (lines 49-56) is that:

options.A: candles, coal, oil, and gas are all sources of carbon and hydrogen.
options.B: the processes of combustion and respiration are governed by the same universal laws of conservation and chemical change.
options.C: animals, unlike candles, take oxygen from the air and combine it with carbon from food.
options.D: the products of a candle, water vapor and carbon dioxide, are harmful to the atmosphere.

questions.explanationHtml: This paragraph begins by stating, 'See how beautifully everything is balanced. ... Nothing is lost—only changed.' It then connects the specific example of the candle to other forms of combustion and finally to respiration, concluding that 'The same laws... govern' all of them. Option B accurately captures this central idea of universal laws governing change. Option A is too narrow. Option C is an incorrect detail, as the paragraph states they are similar. Option D is not supported by the text.

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following observations, if accurate, would best support the author's claim that the flame burns from vapor and not from the solid wax or the wick alone (lines 20-22)?

options.A: The wax around the wick melts into a small cup of liquid.
options.B: A cold spoon held over the flame becomes clouded with moisture.
options.C: If a candle is blown out, its wick can be relit by igniting the stream of white smoke that rises from it.
options.D: The flame has a dark area at its base, near the wick.

questions.explanationHtml: The author's claim is that the flame exists in the vapor. The experiment described in the third paragraph directly demonstrates this. Blowing out the candle interrupts the burning of the vapor, but the vapor itself is still rising. Relighting this vapor proves that it, not the solid wick or wax, is the fuel source for the flame. Option C is a restatement of this key evidence. The other options describe other properties of the candle but do not directly prove that the flame is in the vapor.

questions.questionHtml: The action of holding a cold spoon over the flame (lines 35-39) most nearly serves to:

options.A: provide a visual demonstration of the formation of water as a byproduct of combustion.
options.B: show that the wax is composed primarily of hydrogen.
options.C: illustrate that the flame is hottest at its outer edge.
options.D: suggest a method for extinguishing a candle by cutting off its air supply.

questions.explanationHtml: The author explicitly states the purpose of this action. Lines 36-37 state that the two main products of burning are 'water and carbonic acid.' Then, 'To show the water, hold a cold spoon... Soon it becomes clouded with moisture, and drops of water collect.' The event is a direct demonstration of the first product, water. This makes option A correct.

questions.questionHtml: Both the second paragraph (lines 6-15) and the fourth paragraph (lines 24-34) are told from the point of view of a narrator who:

options.A: provides a historical overview of scientific discoveries about fire.
options.B: expresses doubt about the validity of the experiments being described.
options.C: guides the reader through a careful observation and analysis of a phenomenon.
options.D: argues passionately against a common but incorrect belief about candle flames.

questions.explanationHtml: Throughout both paragraphs, the narrator uses direct, instructive language ('You see before you...', 'If you look closely...', 'Examine the flame more carefully...') to lead the reader through a step-by-step observation and explanation of what is happening in the candle flame. This is a tutorial or guiding point of view, not a historical (A), doubtful (B), or argumentative (D) one.

questions.questionHtml: It can be reasonably inferred from the final paragraph (lines 57-60) that the passage author views the 'commonest things around us' as:

options.A: deceptive, because their true nature is too complex for most people to understand.
options.B: profound, because careful study of them can reveal fundamental scientific principles.
options.C: trivial, because they are only useful for teaching basic concepts to beginners.
options.D: dangerous, because they can lead to incorrect conclusions if not observed properly.

questions.explanationHtml: The final sentence concludes that studying a simple candle 'teaches us that science begins with careful observation of the commonest things around us.' The entire passage demonstrates that this simple object leads to 'the deepest questions of science.' Therefore, the author finds common things to be profound and instructive. Option B directly captures this idea. Options A, C, and D present attitudes (deceptive, trivial, dangerous) that are not supported by the text's tone of wonder and discovery.

questions.questionHtml: According to the passage, what is the role of the wick in the candle-burning process?

options.A: It is the primary source of the combustible vapor that burns.
options.B: It reacts with the wax to create a hydrocarbon compound.
options.C: It serves as a channel for the liquid wax to travel up to the flame.
options.D: It provides the carbon particles that make the flame bright.

questions.explanationHtml: The second paragraph explains the role of the wick. Lines 11-15 state: 'The heat of the flame melts the solid wax, turning it into liquid, which rises up the wick by capillary action... the wick is surrounded by this molten wax, which creeps up and feeds the flame.' The wick's function is to draw the liquid wax upward via capillary action, making it a channel. Option C accurately describes this. Options A and D are incorrect because the wax, not the wick, is the fuel source and source of carbon. Option B is incorrect; the wax is already a hydrocarbon.

questions.questionHtml: In the context of the passage, the primary purpose of the information presented in the fourth paragraph (lines 27-34) is to:

options.A: contrast the chemical composition of castings with that of ordinary mineral soil.
options.B: describe the specific physical transformation soil undergoes as it passes through a worm.
options.C: explain why larger objects on the surface, such as stones, are never fully buried by worm castings.
options.D: introduce the idea that worms are selective in the types of soil particles they consume.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph details the journey of earth through a worm: being ground in the gizzard, mixed with fluids, and ejected as finely divided castings. It describes the physical transformation of the soil. Option B accurately captures this main idea.

questions.questionHtml: According to the narrator, worms drag leaves and twigs into their burrows for which two primary reasons?

options.A: To line the walls of the burrow and to keep the interior damp.
options.B: To plug the opening for protection and to use them as a source of food.
options.C: To drag in objects to judge their size and to pull them by the base.
options.D: To cover the ground with debris and to accelerate the formation of humus acids.

questions.explanationHtml: The passage explicitly states in lines 36-37: "...partly to plug the openings against cold or enemies, and partly for food." This directly matches option B.

questions.questionHtml: It can be reasonably inferred from the passage that the author uses the phrase "show some judgment" (line 39) primarily to:

options.A: compare the intelligence of worms to that of other lowly creatures.
options.B: suggest that the worms' method of seizing objects is deliberate and effective for their purpose.
options.C: prove that worms are capable of learning from past mistakes when pulling objects.
options.D: criticize the worms for not always choosing the most efficient way to pull an object into the burrow.

questions.explanationHtml: The phrase is immediately followed by an explanation of how they show this judgment: "...generally pulling them by the base or middle to fit the burrow entrance more neatly." This implies their action is not random but is a deliberate method that effectively achieves the goal of getting the object into the burrow. Option B captures this inferred meaning.

questions.questionHtml: The main point of the last paragraph (lines 51-58) is that:

options.A: the most significant effect of worms is the burial of ancient ruins.
options.B: the slow, continuous action of worms has been a major geological force.
options.C: worms are more effective at breaking down rocks than chemical acids are.
options.D: without worms, terrestrial life would not be able to survive on earth.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph begins with "The action of worms is slow but continuous" and then lists large-scale effects like leveling land, burying ruins, and transforming subsoil. It concludes by stating they have "exerted a powerful influence on the earth's history." Option B accurately summarizes this as a major geological force.

questions.questionHtml: According to the passage, what is one observable sign that worms are most active after rainfall?

options.A: The worms retire deep into the soil, leaving no trace on the surface.
options.B: The burrow openings are neatly plugged with leaves and small twigs.
options.C: Their castings are particularly abundant, forming small heaps or towers.
options.D: The mineral particles from below are brought up and blended with organic remains.

questions.explanationHtml: Lines 15-17 state: "...the castings are so abundant that they form small heaps or towers, especially after rain, when the worms are most active." This directly provides the evidence asked for in the question.

questions.questionHtml: What evidence, if accurate, would best support the author's claim that "worms pass vast quantities of soil through their bodies every year" (lines 24-25)?

options.A: Observations that castings are finely divided because they have passed through the worm's gizzard.
options.B: The fact that worms drag leaves into their burrows, which then decay and contribute to the mould.
options.C: The estimate that in good pasture land, the annual layer of earth brought to the surface could be more than two inches thick.
options.D: The statement that in fields long undisturbed, large stones sink slowly into the earth.

questions.explanationHtml: The author makes the claim about vast quantities of soil, and the preceding sentence (lines 20-23) provides the evidence: "One observer calculated that on good pasture land, the annual amount of earth thus brought to the surface might equal a layer more than two inches thick over the whole area." This quantitative estimate directly supports the claim of vast quantities.

questions.questionHtml: Based on the passage, the author would most likely agree with which of the following statements about earthworms?

options.A: Their role in soil formation is significant but ultimately secondary to chemical processes like decay.
options.B: Their lowly status in the animal kingdom is deserved, as their actions are purely mechanical and instinctual.
options.C: Their contribution to the fertility of the earth is out of proportion to their small size and humble nature.
options.D: Their activity is most remarkable for the speed with which they can transform a barren landscape.

questions.explanationHtml: The author consistently highlights the massive, planet-altering effects of these "lowly creatures" (line 9) and "small, silent laborers" (line 56). This contrast between their humble nature and their powerful influence supports option C. Option D is incorrect because the author emphasizes their action is "slow but continuous" (line 51), not fast.

questions.questionHtml: In the context of the passage as a whole, the description of worms dragging in "triangles of paper or thin leaves" (lines 38-39) most nearly serves to:

options.A: provide a specific, observable example of the "judgment" worms exhibit in their behavior.
options.B: illustrate the type of organic debris that is most resistant to decay.
options.C: contrast the artificial materials found near gardens with natural leaves and twigs.
options.D: explain why worms are most active at night when they can perform these complex tasks.

questions.explanationHtml: This specific detail is presented immediately after the statement that worms "show some judgment" in how they seize objects. The paper and leaves are examples of the objects they pull in, and the way they do it demonstrates the judgment mentioned, making option A the correct purpose.

questions.questionHtml: According to the narrator, how do worms make the walls of their burrows smooth and firm?

options.A: By dragging leaves and twigs across the surface to polish the entrance.
options.B: By mixing digestive fluids with the soil before excreting it.
options.C: By pressing their bodies firmly against the walls as they move up and down.
options.D: By lining the burrows with their own castings.

questions.explanationHtml: Lines 44-45 state: "Burrows themselves are lined with castings, making smooth, firm walls." This directly indicates that they use castings to line the burrows.

questions.questionHtml: In the context of the passage, the event described in the fifth paragraph (lines 61-77) most nearly serves to:

options.A: contrast the organized efforts of the government with the chaotic efforts of private aid societies.
options.B: criticize Pierce's Port Royal experiment for failing to address the broader crisis.
options.C: illustrate the first organized, official attempt to address the refugee problem, which then inspired broader action.
options.D: highlight the Treasury Department's success in managing the situation before the army intervened.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph details how Pierce was officially tasked to study conditions, started the Port Royal experiment, and his efforts, along with the growing crisis, led to the formation of the many Freedmen's Aid societies mentioned in the next paragraph. It shows a progression from an initial official act to a wider movement.

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following statements best captures how the narrator of the passage feels about the way the initial military policies treated the fugitive slaves?

options.A: He is satisfied that General Butler's practical solution was the only logical approach.
options.B: He is critical of the inconsistency and lack of a coherent, humane policy.
options.C: He believes the army acted correctly by prioritizing military resources over humanitarian concerns.
options.D: He is indifferent, viewing the policies as a minor detail in the larger context of the war.

questions.explanationHtml: The narrator presents the different, contradictory policies of Butler and Halleck without endorsing either. He notes that the issue "complicated rather than solved the problem," highlighting the confusion and lack of a clear direction, which implies a critical view of the disjointed and often inhumane responses.

questions.questionHtml: According to the narrator, the Emancipation Proclamation and the War Amendments had what effect on the "Negro problems"?

options.A: They solved the immediate military issues regarding fugitive slaves.
options.B: They broadened and intensified the difficulties.
options.C: They were largely ignored by the army and the public.
options.D: They clearly defined the government's plan for Reconstruction.

questions.explanationHtml: The passage explicitly states in lines 13-15: "the Emancipation Proclamation seemed but to broaden and intensify the difficulties; and the War Amendments made the Negro problems of to- day."

questions.questionHtml: It can reasonably be inferred from the passage that General Halleck's command to "deliver" fugitive slaves to their owners (lines 36-39) was:

options.A: based on a clear understanding of the complexities on the ground.
options.B: widely popular and easily implemented by Union soldiers.
options.C: difficult to enforce and contradicted by the reality of the refugees' status.
options.D: the official policy that eventually led to the formation of the Freedmen's Bureau.

questions.explanationHtml: The passage immediately follows Halleck's command by stating, "Such a policy was difficult to enforce; some of the black refugees declared themselves freemen, others showed that their masters had deserted them, and still others were captured with forts and plantations." This directly supports the inference that reality made the order impractical.

questions.questionHtml: Both the statement from Secretary Cameron ("They constitute a military resource...") and the actions of General Butler and General Fremont are presented by the narrator as:

options.A: responses that acknowledged the growing significance of slaves to the war effort.
options.B: evidence of a unified national strategy regarding emancipation.
options.C: humanitarian efforts to alleviate the suffering of the freedmen.
options.D: actions that were immediately approved and supported by Congress and President Lincoln.

questions.explanationHtml: Butler saw slaves as "contraband of war" to be used, Fremont freed them as a war measure, and Cameron explicitly called them a "military resource." All three perspectives, despite their differences, view enslaved people through the lens of their impact on the war, acknowledging their strategic importance.

questions.questionHtml: The main point of the last paragraph (lines 88-95) is that:

options.A: the freedmen were lazy and thoughtless with their new freedom.
options.B: the chaotic situation of the freedmen revealed a deeper, long-term economic crisis.
options.C: local efforts were more effective than government intervention in solving labor issues.
options.D: the primary problem facing the freedmen was a lack of moral guidance.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph states it was "plain that this was no ordinary matter of temporary relief, but a national crisis; for here loomed a labor problem of vast dimensions." The examples of idleness and squandering pay are used as evidence of this deeper, systemic economic problem, not as the main point itself.

questions.questionHtml: What evidence from the passage, if accurate, would best support the narrator's claim that the Freedmen's Aid societies were "born of the touching appeals from Pierce and from these other centres of distress" (lines 78-79)?

options.A: The societies sent clothes, money, school-books, and teachers southward.
options.B: The destitution of the freedmen was often reported as "too appalling for belief."
options.C: Army chaplains found new and fruitful fields as superintendents of contrabands.
options.D: Pierce was a firm friend of Secretary Chase and was specially detailed to study conditions.

questions.explanationHtml: The narrator's claim is that the societies were formed in response to distress. The evidence that best supports this is the report of the dire conditions, the "destitution... 'too appalling for belief." This would be the motivating "appeal" that spurred people to action. The other options describe actions taken or positions held, not the motivating distress itself.

questions.questionHtml: It can reasonably be inferred that the passage author viewed the statement by Congress, the President, and the Nation that "The war has naught to do with slaves" (line 22) as:

options.A: a correct reflection of the political aims of the Union at the start of the war.
options.B: an ironic statement, immediately contradicted by the unfolding events on the ground.
options.C: the primary reason for the slow formation of the Freedmen's Bureau.
options.D: a necessary political stance to maintain unity in the North.

questions.explanationHtml: The narrator presents the official denial ("The war has naught to do with slaves") and then immediately undercuts it with "and yet no sooner had the armies... penetrated Virginia and Tennessee than fugitive slaves appeared." This contrast creates an ironic effect, showing how reality proved the official statement false.

questions.questionHtml: According to the passage, how did Secretary Cameron characterize the enslaved people in late 1861?

options.A: As a group of starving vagabonds in dark distress.
options.B: As a source of political controversy between the North and South.
options.C: As a military resource that should not be turned over to the enemy.
options.D: As a labor pool for the Port Royal experiment in making free workingmen.

questions.explanationHtml: The passage directly quotes Secretary Cameron in lines 44-46: "They constitute a military resource," wrote Secretary Cameron, late in 1861; "and being such, that they should not be turned over to the enemy is too plain to discuss."

questions.questionHtml: In the context of the passage, the event described in the third paragraph (lines 26-38) most nearly serves to:

options.A: illustrate the widow's genuine affection for Huck through her emotional reaction to his return.
options.B: emphasize the widow's religious piety and her attempts to teach Huck proper grace before meals.
options.C: highlight the contrast between the freedom Huck desires and the restrictive nature of the widow's domestic life.
options.D: explain the widow's unique and superior method of cooking, which involves keeping all food items separate.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph details Huck's discomfort in his new clothes ("sweat and feel all cramped up") and the strict rules at the widow's, like the supper bell and the "grumble" over food. Huck contrasts this with the appeal of eating from a "barrel of odds and ends," where things "get mixed up" and "go better." This directly highlights the conflict between his love of freedom and the widow's restrictive "civilizing."

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following statements best captures how the narrator of the passage feels about the widow's prohibition on smoking?

options.A: He respects her authority and is genuinely convinced that smoking is a dirty habit.
options.B: He finds it hypocritical because she disapproves of his harmless habits while engaging in her own, like taking snuff.
options.C: He is confused by the rule because he believes smoking has benefits and the widow cannot provide a valid reason for the ban.
options.D: He is indifferent to the rule, as he plans to run away again and smoke whenever he wants.

questions.explanationHtml: Huck points out the inconsistency in the widow's logic. She forbids him from smoking, a practice he feels has "some good in it," while she herself takes snuff. He observes the double standard with the sarcastic remark, "of course that was all right, because she done it herself." This reveals his feeling that her rule is hypocritical.

questions.questionHtml: According to the narrator of the passage, the money from the cave made him and Tom Sawyer rich, and Judge Thatcher managed it in a way that:

options.A: gave them a small but steady income of a dollar a day each.
options.B: required them to wait until they were adults to access the full amount.
options.C: was risky but ultimately profitable, making them six thousand dollars apiece.
options.D: allowed them to use the money to start a band of robbers.

questions.explanationHtml: The passage states explicitly in lines 15-16: "Judge Thatcher he took it and put it out at interest, and it fetched us a dollar a day apiece all the year round." This is a direct detail about how the money was managed and the result.

questions.questionHtml: It can reasonably be inferred from the passage that the narrator's primary reason for returning to live with the Widow Douglas was that:

options.A: he genuinely missed the comfort and security of living in a proper house.
options.B: he felt guilty for running away and causing the widow to cry over him.
options.C: the widow promised to relax her rules and allow him to smoke if he came back.
options.D: he was enticed by the opportunity to join Tom Sawyer's proposed robber band.

questions.explanationHtml: Lines 23-25 state: "But Tom Sawyer he hunted me up and said he was going to start a band of robbers, and I might join if I would go back to the widow and be respectable. So I went back." This directly links his return to the condition for joining the band, implying that this was his primary motivation.

questions.questionHtml: The main point of the fourth paragraph (lines 39-43) is that:

options.A: Huck is eager to learn about biblical figures like Moses.
options.B: Huck loses interest in Moses once he learns Moses is dead and therefore irrelevant to him.
options.C: the widow's teaching methods are ineffective because she cannot answer Huck's questions.
options.D: Huck believes the story of Moses is more interesting than the widow's other lessons.

questions.explanationHtml: The paragraph describes Huck's initial curiosity ("I was in a sweat to find out all about him") which completely vanishes the moment he discovers Moses is dead ("so then I didn't care no more about him"). His stated reason, "I don't take no stock in dead people," is the central idea of the paragraph.

questions.questionHtml: What evidence from the passage best supports the author's claim that the widow's ways made Huck feel "all cramped up" (line 30)?

options.A: "The Widow Douglas she took me for her son, and allowed she would sivilize me."
options.B: "She put me in them new clothes again, and I couldn't do nothing but sweat and sweat."
options.C: "After supper she got out her book and learned me about Moses and the Bulrushers."
options.D: "And she took snuff, too; of course that was all right, because she done it herself."

questions.explanationHtml: The question asks for evidence of feeling "cramped up." The phrase is used immediately after describing his discomfort in his new clothes. Option B directly quotes the sentence where he describes the physical sensation of being confined and sweaty in the restrictive clothing, which serves as a metaphor for the broader constraints he feels.

questions.questionHtml: It can reasonably be inferred that the narrator uses the word "stretched" (line 4) and "stretchers" (line 10) mainly to mean:

options.A: exaggerations or untruths.
options.B: physical descriptions of a character.
options.C: important plot points that extend the story.
options.D: long-winded explanations.

questions.explanationHtml: Huck is discussing the truthfulness of "The Adventures of Tom Sawyer." He says the book told "the truth, mainly," but that there were things which he "stretched." This is immediately followed by the observation that everyone lies occasionally, establishing a contrast with telling the truth. In this context, "stretched" and "stretchers" are colloquial terms for lies or exaggerations.

questions.questionHtml: According to the narrator, the widow's prayer or "grumble" over the food before supper (lines 33-34) was unwelcome because:

options.A: it made him feel guilty for running away.
options.B: it forced him to wait before he could eat.
options.C: it was a criticism of the quality of the cooking.
options.D: it was a long and boring ritual that he did not understand.

questions.explanationHtml: The passage states in lines 31-33: "When you got to the table you couldn't go right to eating, but you had to wait for the widow to tuck down her head and grumble a little over the victuals." The cause of his frustration is the waiting, not the content of the prayer itself.

questions.questionHtml: Which of the following statements best summarizes the narrator's critique in lines 46-52 ("That is just the way... because she done it herself")?

options.A: People often criticize harmless activities they don't understand while overlooking their own questionable habits.
options.B: The widow should focus on teaching about living people instead of dead biblical figures like Moses.
options.C: It is unfair for the widow to prevent him from enjoying the simple pleasure of smoking a pipe.
options.D: The widow is a generally good person whose small flaws, like taking snuff, should be overlooked.

questions.explanationHtml: In this passage, Huck makes a general statement about "some people" who "get down on a thing when they don't know nothing about it." He then provides a specific example: the widow criticizes his smoking (which has "some good in it") while she uses snuff. The core of his critique is the human tendency toward hypocrisy and judging things one is ignorant of, which is best captured in option A.
